g.data-narration: Welcome to Decision Tree Learning! Let's explore this intuitive and powerful machine learning model.
A decision tree creates a flowchart-like model where each node tests a feature, branches show outcomes, and leaves provide predictions.
g.text: A supervised learning model that creates a tree structure where:
• Each internal node tests a feature
• Branches represent outcomes of the test

g.data-narration: The four main components are: Root node (top), Internal nodes (tests), Branches (outcomes), and Leaf nodes (predictions).
The root node is the starting point. The algorithm selects the most informative feature using metrics like information gain or Gini impurity.
g.text: The topmost node representing the first decision. Selected using the most informative feature.
• Selects feature that best separates classes

g.data-narration: Internal nodes test specific features. Examples include: Is temperature greater than 30°C? or Is color equal to Red?
g.text: Each internal node represents a test on an attribute:

g.data-narration: Branches represent the outcomes of each test. Binary splits have two branches (yes/no), while categorical features may have multiple branches.
In classification trees, leaf nodes contain class labels - the majority class of training samples reaching that leaf.
g.text: Leaf nodes contain class labels (majority class of training samples reaching this leaf)
2. Follow branches based on feature values

g.data-narration: Regression trees predict continuous values. Leaf nodes contain the mean value of training samples reaching that leaf.
g.text: Leaf nodes contain predicted values (mean of training samples reaching this leaf)
ŷ = mean(yᵢ for samples in leaf)

g.data-narration: Classification trees predict categories using Gini/Entropy, while regression trees predict continuous values using variance reduction.
Decision trees use a top-down greedy approach: start at root, select best feature to split, repeat recursively until stopping criteria met.
g.text: How Decision Trees Learn - Top-Down Approach
⚠ Greedy: Locally optimal splits may not be globally optimal

g.data-narration: Gini impurity measures node purity. Formula: Gini = 1 - Σ(pᵢ)² where pᵢ is probability of class i. Zero means pure node.
g.text: pᵢ = probability of class i in the node

g.data-narration: Entropy measures uncertainty. Formula: Entropy = -Σ(pᵢ × log₂(pᵢ)). Information Gain = parent entropy - weighted child entropy.
g.text: 📌 Choose split that maximizes Information Gain (or minimizes weighted Gini)

g.data-narration: Trees stop splitting when: node is pure, max depth reached, minimum samples met, or no further information gain possible.
g.text: The tree stops splitting when any of these conditions are met:

g.data-narration: Decision trees are 'white box' models - every prediction can be explained by tracing the path from root to leaf. Crucial for healthcare, finance, and law.
g.text: Why Decision Trees Are Important - Interpretability

g.data-narration: Advantages: interpretable, no preprocessing, handles mixed data, non-parametric. Limitations: overfitting, instability, axis-parallel splits, greedy nature.
